3: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Animal foods are important sources of nutrients, such as vi- tamins and minerals, and concentrate a significant amount of amino acids essential for maintaining human health (De Smet and Vossen, 2016). In addition, the increase in income and in the global urban population, among other factors, have transformed the way people eat, increasing the demand for proteins of animal origin in recent decades (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012; Boland et al., 2013). In this context, poultry meat is considered a driving force in the supply of animal protein for the coming decades, as it is
produced using relatively fast production cycle systems, is efficient in feed conversion, and requires little land use during the animal breeding stage (OECD, 2015; FAO, 2016).

Brazil is the largest exporter of poultry meat in the world and the second in production, competing directly with the United States and China. Such production is mainly carried out in the southern region of Brazil (62%) (ABPA, 2018). Of its production, on average 70% is consumed by the domestic market and 30% is destined for export (ABPA, 2018; Valdes et al., 2015). The process of integration of the production chain is led preferably by com- panies or cooperatives. Different production systems are adopted, with a more or less intense use of technologies and characteris- tics of organic or conventional production (Valdes et al., 2015). On the other hand, aspects observed in intensive poultry farming, as is the case in Brazil, indicate a potential of environmental impacts related to natural resources such as air, water and soil in the sites
where the productive systems are installed (Steinfeld et al., 2006; Gerber et al., 2007; Macleod et al., 2013).

Gases and residues emitted during the breeding of chickens contribute to the greenhouse effect, such as methane from manure management and ammonia from the decomposition of excreta. An- other aggravating factor refers to changes in land use caused by the demand for grains used for animal feed, which are also important sources of emissions (Gerber et al., 2007; Macleod et al., 2013).

This demand for land can modify ecosystems and reduce nat- ural habitats, putting biodiversity at risk. It is estimated that 30% of the area where there is no ice on the planet is used for graz- ing and 33% of the arable land is used to produce animal feed (Aiking, 2011; Foley et al., 2011). Therefore, it is predicted that the proportion of arable lands to produce animal feed cereals will con- tinue to increase (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012). Thus, the an- thropic occupation, which has introduced significant amounts of pollutants in the water, air and soil by conventional agriculture and livestock, tends to worsen the environmental scenario of pro- ducing countries if there is no sustainable intensification with a proper management of water and fertilizers, thus blocking agri- cultural expansion and reducing food waste (Mueller et al., 2012; Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012; Godfray et al., 2010).

As a result of such current and future challenges, several studies have been carried out in recent years seeking to an- alyze mainly the environmental impacts on the poultry sector in several countries. As examples, we cite Norway, the United States of America, Italy, United Kingdom, Australia, Brazil/France, Portugal, France and Australia (Ellingsen and Aanondsen, 2006; Pelletier, 2008; Boggia et al., 2010; Castellini et al., 2012; Leinonen et al., 2012; Leinonen et al., 2013; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Prudêncio da Silva et al., 2014; Van der Werf and Salou 2015; Cesari et al., 2016; Paolotti et al., 2016; and Wiedemann et al., 2016, respectively). However, these thirteen studies evaluate only the environmental impacts of the production systems of this animal protein, disregarding the economic perspective, mak- ing eco-efficiency analysis impossible. The studies by López- Andrés et al. (2018), carried out in Mexico, and Rocchi et al. (2019), carried out in Italy, have analyzed both the environmental and the economic impacts on poultry production systems, applying statisti- cal methods in order to increase the reliability of the results. How- ever, simultaneous analysis (environmental and economic) is still incipient.

Therefore, seeking to jointly integrate the performance of a sys- tem or a product, in the late 1980s, Schaltegger and Sturm intro- duced the term eco-efficiency as a cross-relation between socio- economic rationality and ecological issues, associating environ- mental impacts with the value of production of goods and ser- vices (Ehrenfeld, 2005; Huppes and Ishikawa, 2005; Kicherer et al., 2007). One of the main objectives of eco-efficiency is the cor- porate environmental management operated at various levels of an organizational structure and applied to different projects (Kicherer et al., 2007), enabling i) maximizing the results of an economic resource, ii) increasing ecological efficiency interpreted from a purely environmental view, and iii) crossing the previous items, which can be understood as economic-ecological efficiency resulting from the measurement of the added environmental im- pacts caused by the generated monetary unit.

Thus, there was a need to measure the eco-efficiency of pro- duction systems, whereas eco-efficiency is a management tool that aims to create value while improving the environmental and eco- nomic performance of products or services along the entire pro- duction chain in parallel with its value. It can still contribute to an- alyze which system or product is less unsustainable (ABNT, 2014).
Thereby, no study has been found linking life cycle assessment (LCA) and economic value added (EVA) methodologies to analyze environmental and economic performance. Mainly measuring the
eco-efficiency in poultry production systems, namely positive pres- sure (PP), dark house (DH), and organic systems (ORG), in the South region of Brazil,

These methodologies are qualified of assessing eco-efficiency. LCA estimates the environmental impacts (e.g. positive or nega- tive) that products or services carry from their source to their final disposal. LCA uses a holistic view of the entire supply chain that supplies a given product system and responsibly attributes to it a fraction of the environmental loads from other production systems (Guinée et al. 2011; Hauschild et al., 2013). On the other hand, the basic principle of EVA is to evaluate the performance of projects based on the economic value that managers add to the business and return to the owners or shareholders. Unlike traditional mea- sures (eg return on investment, earnings per share, return on net assets and weighted average profit) of financial performance, EVA links a company's book value to its real market value, adjusting its operating profit net and subtracting the amount of money used to finance a project at the weighted average cost of each fraction of that invested capital (Malmi; Ikäheimo, 2003; Stern value manage- ment, 2018).

Therefore, the goal of the study was to evaluate the eco- efficiency of different poultry production systems. For this, we specifically seek: a) inventory and evaluate the environmental per- formance of the production of one kg of live weight of chicken; b) calculate the added economic value of the production of one kg of live weight of chicken; c) compare the economic and environmen- tal indicators of the types of systems investigated using life cycle assessment and economic value added.

This study, in addition to this introduction, is structured as fol- lows: in section 2, the methodological procedures are described by describing the study area, the life cycle assessment and the eco- nomic value added methods, as well as the sensitivity analysis and the Monte Carlo simulation; in sections 3 and 4, the results, dis- cussions and conclusions are presented, ending by explaining the main limitations of the study, as well as recommendations and suggestions for future lines of research on the topic addressed.

One of the main objectives of eco-efficiency is the cor- porate environmental management operated at various levels of an organizational structure and applied to different projects (Kicherer et al., 2007), enabling i) maximizing the results of an economic resource, ii) increasing ecological efficiency interpreted from a purely environmental view, and iii) crossing the previous items, which can be understood as economic-ecological efficiency resulting from the measurement of the added environmental im- pacts caused by the generated monetary unit.

These methodologies are qualified of assessing eco-efficiency. LCA estimates the environmental impacts (e.g. positive or nega- tive) that products or services carry from their source to their final disposal. LCA uses a holistic view of the entire supply chain that supplies a given product system and responsibly attributes to it a fraction of the environmental loads from other production systems (Guinée et al. 2011; Hauschild et al., 2013). On the other hand, the basic principle of EVA is to evaluate the performance of projects based on the economic value that managers add to the business and return to the owners or shareholders. Unlike traditional mea- sures (eg return on investment, earnings per share, return on net assets and weighted average profit) of financial performance, EVA links a company's book value to its real market value, adjusting its operating profit net and subtracting the amount of money used to finance a project at the weighted average cost of each fraction of that invested capital (Malmi; Ikäheimo, 2003; Stern value manage- ment, 2018).

Therefore, the goal of the study was to evaluate the eco- efficiency of different poultry production systems. For this, we specifically seek: a) inventory and evaluate the environmental per- formance of the production of one kg of live weight of chicken; b) calculate the added economic value of the production of one kg of live weight of chicken; c) compare the economic and environmen- tal indicators of the types of systems investigated using life cycle assessment and economic value added.

This study, in addition to this introduction, is structured as fol- lows: in section 2, the methodological procedures are described by describing the study area, the life cycle assessment and the eco- nomic value added methods, as well as the sensitivity analysis and the Monte Carlo simulation; in sections 3 and 4, the results, dis- cussions and conclusions are presented, ending by explaining the main limitations of the study, as well as recommendations and suggestions for future lines of research on the topic addressed.

In addition, the increase in income and in the global urban population, among other factors, have transformed the way people eat, increasing the demand for proteins of animal origin in recent decades (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012; Boland et al., 2013). In this context, poultry meat is considered a driving force in the supply of animal protein for the coming decades, as it is
produced using relatively fast production cycle systems, is efficient in feed conversion, and requires little land use during the animal breeding stage (OECD, 2015; FAO, 2016).

Of its production, on average 70% is consumed by the domestic market and 30% is destined for export (ABPA, 2018; Valdes et al., 2015). Gases and residues emitted during the breeding of chickens contribute to the greenhouse effect, such as methane from manure management and ammonia from the decomposition of excreta. An- other aggravating factor refers to changes in land use caused by the demand for grains used for animal feed, which are also important sources of emissions (Gerber et al., 2007; Macleod et al., 2013).

This demand for land can modify ecosystems and reduce nat- ural habitats, putting biodiversity at risk. It is estimated that 30% of the area where there is no ice on the planet is used for graz- ing and 33% of the arable land is used to produce animal feed (Aiking, 2011; Foley et al., 2011). Therefore, it is predicted that the proportion of arable lands to produce animal feed cereals will con- tinue to increase (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012). Thus, the an- thropic occupation, which has introduced significant amounts of pollutants in the water, air and soil by conventional agriculture and livestock, tends to worsen the environmental scenario of pro- ducing countries if there is no sustainable intensification with a proper management of water and fertilizers, thus blocking agri- cultural expansion and reducing food waste (Mueller et al., 2012; Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012; Godfray et al., 2010).

In addition, the increase in income and in the global urban population, among other factors, have transformed the way people eat, increasing the demand for proteins of animal origin in recent decades (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012; Boland et al., 2013). In this context, poultry meat is considered a driving force in the supply of animal protein for the coming decades, as it is
produced using relatively fast production cycle systems, is efficient in feed conversion, and requires little land use during the animal breeding stage (OECD, 2015; FAO, 2016).

Brazil is the largest exporter of poultry meat in the world and the second in production, competing directly with the United States and China. Such production is mainly carried out in the southern region of Brazil (62%) (ABPA, 2018). Of its production, on average 70% is consumed by the domestic market and 30% is destined for export (ABPA, 2018; Valdes et al., 2015). The process of integration of the production chain is led preferably by com- panies or cooperatives. Different production systems are adopted, with a more or less intense use of technologies and characteris- tics of organic or conventional production (Valdes et al., 2015). On the other hand, aspects observed in intensive poultry farming, as is the case in Brazil, indicate a potential of environmental impacts related to natural resources such as air, water and soil in the sites
where the productive systems are installed (Steinfeld et al., 2006; Gerber et al., 2007; Macleod et al., 2013).

Gases and residues emitted during the breeding of chickens contribute to the greenhouse effect, such as methane from manure management and ammonia from the decomposition of excreta. An- other aggravating factor refers to changes in land use caused by the demand for grains used for animal feed, which are also important sources of emissions (Gerber et al., 2007; Macleod et al., 2013).

This demand for land can modify ecosystems and reduce nat- ural habitats, putting biodiversity at risk. It is estimated that 30% of the area where there is no ice on the planet is used for graz- ing and 33% of the arable land is used to produce animal feed (Aiking, 2011; Foley et al., 2011). Therefore, it is predicted that the proportion of arable lands to produce animal feed cereals will con- tinue to increase (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012). Thus, the an- thropic occupation, which has introduced significant amounts of pollutants in the water, air and soil by conventional agriculture and livestock, tends to worsen the environmental scenario of pro- ducing countries if there is no sustainable intensification with a proper management of water and fertilizers, thus blocking agri- cultural expansion and reducing food waste (Mueller et al., 2012; Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012; Godfray et al., 2010).

As a result of such current and future challenges, several studies have been carried out in recent years seeking to an- alyze mainly the environmental impacts on the poultry sector in several countries. As examples, we cite Norway, the United States of America, Italy, United Kingdom, Australia, Brazil/France, Portugal, France and Australia (Ellingsen and Aanondsen, 2006; Pelletier, 2008; Boggia et al., 2010; Castellini et al., 2012; Leinonen et al., 2012; Leinonen et al., 2013; Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Prudêncio da Silva et al., 2014; Van der Werf and Salou 2015; Cesari et al., 2016; Paolotti et al., 2016; and Wiedemann et al., 2016, respectively). However, these thirteen studies evaluate only the environmental impacts of the production systems of this animal protein, disregarding the economic perspective, mak- ing eco-efficiency analysis impossible. The studies by López- Andrés et al. (2018), carried out in Mexico, and Rocchi et al. (2019), carried out in Italy, have analyzed both the environmental and the economic impacts on poultry production systems, applying statisti- cal methods in order to increase the reliability of the results. How- ever, simultaneous analysis (environmental and economic) is still incipient.

Therefore, seeking to jointly integrate the performance of a sys- tem or a product, in the late 1980s, Schaltegger and Sturm intro- duced the term eco-efficiency as a cross-relation between socio- economic rationality and ecological issues, associating environ- mental impacts with the value of production of goods and ser- vices (Ehrenfeld, 2005; Huppes and Ishikawa, 2005; Kicherer et al., 2007). One of the main objectives of eco-efficiency is the cor- porate environmental management operated at various levels of an organizational structure and applied to different projects (Kicherer et al., 2007), enabling i) maximizing the results of an economic resource, ii) increasing ecological efficiency interpreted from a purely environmental view, and iii) crossing the previous items, which can be understood as economic-ecological efficiency resulting from the measurement of the added environmental im- pacts caused by the generated monetary unit.

Thus, there was a need to measure the eco-efficiency of pro- duction systems, whereas eco-efficiency is a management tool that aims to create value while improving the environmental and eco- nomic performance of products or services along the entire pro- duction chain in parallel with its value. It can still contribute to an- alyze which system or product is less unsustainable (ABNT, 2014).
Thereby, no study has been found linking life cycle assessment (LCA) and economic value added (EVA) methodologies to analyze environmental and economic performance. Mainly measuring the
eco-efficiency in poultry production systems, namely positive pres- sure (PP), dark house (DH), and organic systems (ORG), in the South region of Brazil,

These methodologies are qualified of assessing eco-efficiency. LCA estimates the environmental impacts (e.g. positive or nega- tive) that products or services carry from their source to their final disposal. LCA uses a holistic view of the entire supply chain that supplies a given product system and responsibly attributes to it a fraction of the environmental loads from other production systems (Guinée et al. 2011; Hauschild et al., 2013). On the other hand, the basic principle of EVA is to evaluate the performance of projects based on the economic value that managers add to the business and return to the owners or shareholders. Unlike traditional mea- sures (eg return on investment, earnings per share, return on net assets and weighted average profit) of financial performance, EVA links a company's book value to its real market value, adjusting its operating profit net and subtracting the amount of money used to finance a project at the weighted average cost of each fraction of that invested capital (Malmi; Ikäheimo, 2003; Stern value manage- ment, 2018).

Therefore, the goal of the study was to evaluate the eco- efficiency of different poultry production systems. For this, we specifically seek: a) inventory and evaluate the environmental per- formance of the production of one kg of live weight of chicken; b) calculate the added economic value of the production of one kg of live weight of chicken; c) compare the economic and environmen- tal indicators of the types of systems investigated using life cycle assessment and economic value added.

This study, in addition to this introduction, is structured as fol- lows: in section 2, the methodological procedures are described by describing the study area, the life cycle assessment and the eco- nomic value added methods, as well as the sensitivity analysis and the Monte Carlo simulation; in sections 3 and 4, the results, dis- cussions and conclusions are presented, ending by explaining the main limitations of the study, as well as recommendations and suggestions for future lines of research on the topic addressed.
12: The methodological procedures of this study go through the fol- lowing steps: i) analysis of the environmental impacts of produc- tion systems using the methodological framework of the life cy- cle assessment (LCA) (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006), ii) eval- uation of the economic performance of production systems using the economic value added (EVA) method, iii) measurement of eco- efficiency (ISO 14045; ABNT, 2014), iv) reduction of uncertainties regarding the estimates of EVA/kg calculated in different produc- tion systems through sensitivity analysis and Monte Carlo simula- tion. It is worth noting that Supplementary Information (SI) (see. Table S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, S6, S7, S8, S9, S10, S11, S12) provides es- sential data for understanding the results.
13: Brazilian broiler production is concentrated in the southern re- gion. The state of Paraná is the largest producer, followed by Santa Catarina and Rio Grande do Sul (ABPA, 2018). Most of the pro- duction is carried out in high productivity housing systems, more precisely the positive pressure (PP) and the dark house (DH) sys- items. In addition, organic systems (ORG) represent a growing mar- ket niche in Brazil. In the evaluated organic systems, the final prod- uct follows a single weight standard for slaughter, i.e., an average of 3.3 kg. In high production systems, however, birds with differ- ent weights are produced according to the integrating company's market focus. The study considers the production of Griller, Broiler
-56.000|-52.000|-48.000|-44.000 Rio South region [ ] States|Paraná Santa Catarina Grande do Sul 0 100|200 300 km| -56.000|-52.000|-48.000|-44.000
and Heavy chickens, with average slaughter weights of 1.4, 2.9 and 3.5 kg, respectively.

Given the concentration of the production in the south of the country and the convenience for data collection, this study inves- tigated the production of the states of Paraná and Santa Catarina (Fig. 1). In Paraná, the data for the DH and PP systems were ob- tained from the database of the Federation of Farmers of Paraná (FAEP) on the following municipalities: Castro, Toledo, Londrina, Dois Vizinhos, Chopinzinho, Cambará, and Cascavel. In Santa Cata- rina, data for the ORG system were collected in the municipality of Santa Rosa de Lima.

The FAEP database is constructed by panels with producers and technicians who represent the typical farm in the inventoried mu- nicipalities. The database is made up of data referring to produc- tion using the DH and PP systems, collecting zootechnical and production indexes from 125 lots over a three-year period (2015- 2017). The use of this database made it possible to conduct a ro- bust LCA linked to the economic evaluation of the systems.

In addition, data regarding poultry production using the organic system were collected directly from producers in the municipality of Santa Rosa de Lima, state of Santa Catarina. Semi-structured in- terviews with producers and processors were conducted in 2017. The interviews were directed in order to collect the data necessary to conduct the LCA and the economic evaluation. More precisely, data on the structure used for production, zootechnical indexes, in- gredients and formulations of feed, origin of inputs, and economic indicators (prices, costs and investments) were obtained.

The PP systems, also known as conventional, are semi-climatic systems and are considered intensive. They have an average area of 1200 m² and can house 10-18 birds/m² They use little technology compared to automated systems and have fallen out of favor with the growing use of modern and technologically equipped poultry, such as DH. These aviaries use PP fans to renew the air inside the house and provide a feeling of thermal comfort to animals. Feeders can be tubular, automatic and pendant, or nipple drinkers. In this system, the control of environmental thermal conditions is less in-

tense. Yellow, blue, white or reflective raffia curtains are adopted and do not have an internal cooling and heating system. The types can be bell, wood or electric heaters (Abreu and Abreu, 2011). The negative pressure DH poultry houses enable a control of in- ternal environment factors, isolating them from externalities, with an average area of 2400 m². The shed is closed and the level of automation in these systems is higher than in the others used commercially in Brazil. Dark curtains and seals prevent light and air from entering, and the light control allows regulating the light intensity. Temperature and humidity are measured by sensors di- rectly connected to a control panel that activate equipment such as nebulizers, exhaust fans, evaporative panels, feeders, drinking fountains, and air inlet curtains (Abreu and Abreu, 2011). In the ORG production system, the main difference is not the physical structure, but the inputs used in the breeding process. The birds are restricted as to the use of certain inputs of conventional production and in sanitary management. The inventoried produc- tion systems are similar to PP, however with hatches for chickens to go out for grazing. The aviaries have the capacity to house be- tween 2500 and 6000 chicks; the average is up to ten birds per m². The housing systems are covered by a bed made of eucalyptus shavings with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 8 mm.

17: The tool used for environmental assessment was the life cycle assessment (LCA). This technique estimates the potential environ- mental impacts that products or services may cause from their in- stallation to final disposal, providing a holistic view of the entire supply chain that supplies a given product system and attributing it a fraction of the environmental loads of other production sys- tems in a responsible way (Hauschild et al., 2013).

According to the studies found in the literature regarding the application of LCA to assess poultry, the most used method to as- sess impacts is the CML-IA 2 baseline 2000, and the most ana- lyzed impact categories are climate change, potential acidification,
Table 1 Technical coefficients for poultry production in dark house systems, conventional positive pressure, and organic systems.

DH 2400 Dark house 2400 m²; PP 1200 - Positive pressure 1200 m²; ORG 600 - Positive pressure 600 m². a Technical indicators - Federation of Agriculture of the State of Paraná (FAEP). b Feed ingredients - Brazilian Agricultural Research Corporation (EMBRAPA).

potential eutrophication, and land use changes. Thus, for this study, the choice was the CML-IA 2 baseline 2000, version 4.2, which in- cludes global characterization models for the categories acidifica- tion, eutrophication, and global warming (100yr). The functional unit (FU) refers to one kilogram of chicken in live weight. The ap- proach is called "cradle to gate," since it covers the stages of grain production as raw materials up to the delivery of live chicken by slaughter weight. The steps for transporting inputs and poultry to the slaughterhouse were not considered in this study. It should be noted the investigation is focused on the production process, which shows differences between the types of chickens investi- gated.

For non-organic feed, an animal nutrition expert from Brazil- ian Agricultural Research Corporation (EMBRAPA) pigs and poultry of Concordia/SC was asked to assist in the formulation. The five types of feeds created are of vegetable origin because, according to the expert, many foreign markets do not consume meat from ani- mals fed on animal protein. The feed for the pre-initial, growth 1, growth 2, final 1 and final 2 phases are shown in Supplementary Information (see. Table S13 and S14). The atmospheric emissions from nitrogen flow were estimated using the Tier 2 method of EMEP/EEA air pollutant emission inventory guidebook (Amon et al., 2016). The method estimates atmospheric emissions of ammonia (NH3), nitrous oxide (N20), nitrogen gas (N2), and nitric oxide (NO) from the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) flow of handling animal excreta from a flow approach based on kg of nitrogen. Methane emissions (CH4) were estimated using the Tier 2 method. The method uses emission factors over the rate of production of poultry residues inside the aviary and outside the aviary, when this residue is handled and stored before its application in vegetable crops.

Furthermore, the production coefficients shown in Table 1 were used to quantify the inputs and outputs of raw material and energy from the production systems, an information necessary to identify the results related to the environmental issue considering average data for each item inventoried in all evaluated systems.

For non-organic feed, an animal nutrition expert from Brazil- ian Agricultural Research Corporation (EMBRAPA) pigs and poultry of Concordia/SC was asked to assist in the formulation. The five types of feeds created are of vegetable origin because, according to the expert, many foreign markets do not consume meat from ani- mals fed on animal protein. The feed for the pre-initial, growth 1, growth 2, final 1 and final 2 phases are shown in Supplementary Information (see. Table S13 and S14). The atmospheric emissions from nitrogen flow were estimated using the Tier 2 method of EMEP/EEA air pollutant emission inventory guidebook (Amon et al., 2016). The method estimates atmospheric emissions of ammonia (NH3), nitrous oxide (N20), nitrogen gas (N2), and nitric oxide (NO) from the total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) flow of handling animal excreta from a flow approach based on kg of nitrogen. Methane emissions (CH4) were estimated using the Tier 2 method. The method uses emission factors over the rate of production of poultry residues inside the aviary and outside the aviary, when this residue is handled and stored before its application in vegetable crops.

Furthermore, the production coefficients shown in Table 1 were used to quantify the inputs and outputs of raw material and energy from the production systems, an information necessary to identify the results related to the environmental issue considering average data for each item inventoried in all evaluated systems.
20: The tool used for environmental assessment was the life cycle assessment (LCA). This technique estimates the potential environ- mental impacts that products or services may cause from their in- stallation to final disposal, providing a holistic view of the entire supply chain that supplies a given product system and attributing it a fraction of the environmental loads of other production sys- tems in a responsible way (Hauschild et al., 2013).

According to the studies found in the literature regarding the application of LCA to assess poultry, the most used method to as- sess impacts is the CML-IA 2 baseline 2000, and the most ana- lyzed impact categories are climate change, potential acidification,
Table 1 Technical coefficients for poultry production in dark house systems, conventional positive pressure, and organic systems.

DH 2400 Dark house 2400 m²; PP 1200 - Positive pressure 1200 m²; ORG 600 - Positive pressure 600 m². a Technical indicators - Federation of Agriculture of the State of Paraná (FAEP). b Feed ingredients - Brazilian Agricultural Research Corporation (EMBRAPA).

potential eutrophication, and land use changes. Thus, for this study, the choice was the CML-IA 2 baseline 2000, version 4.2, which in- cludes global characterization models for the categories acidifica- tion, eutrophication, and global warming (100yr). The functional unit (FU) refers to one kilogram of chicken in live weight. The ap- proach is called "cradle to gate," since it covers the stages of grain production as raw materials up to the delivery of live chicken by slaughter weight. The steps for transporting inputs and poultry to the slaughterhouse were not considered in this study. It should be noted the investigation is focused on the production process, which shows differences between the types of chickens investi- gated.

The method uses emission factors over the rate of production of poultry residues inside the aviary and outside the aviary, when this residue is handled and stored before its application in vegetable crops.

Furthermore, the production coefficients shown in Table 1 were used to quantify the inputs and outputs of raw material and energy from the production systems, an information necessary to identify the results related to the environmental issue considering average data for each item inventoried in all evaluated systems.

22: <header>2.3. Evaluation of the value of the product system </header>

The economic value added (EVA) performance measurement was used in order to identify whether the investment made in dif- ferent broiler production systems in the southern region of Brazil creates or destroys economic value for producers. If this mea- sure is positive, there is the creation of economic value; other- wise, the negative value signals the destruction of economic value (Stern value management, 2018).
The EVA is determined by the following expression: EVA = [(NOPAT/IC) X 100] (MARR X IC) (1)
23: EVA economic value added NOPAT net operating profit after taxes IC invested capital MARR minimum attractiveness rate of return

The adjusted hybrid capital asset pricing model (CAPM) was used to estimate minimum attractiveness rate of return (MARR) because, given the asset pricing models that explain how produc- ers assess risk, the CAPM is the most used model in practice as it makes possible to ascertain through a linear relationship between the risk and the return of an investment project, given a level of risk assumed by the producer, the rate of return that rewards this risk, or that is, by taking into account systematic risk (non- diversifiable or market risk), using the Beta coefficient, it is as- sumed to be the most appropriate model for this study (Ross et al., 2002; Póvoa, 2007; Blank et al., 2014; Graham and Harvey, 2001).

Although the CAPM model in its original formulation (Markowitz, 1959; Sharpe, 1964; Lintner, 1965; Mossin, 1966) presents limitations for the application to emerging countries (low expressiveness of capital companies open in the stock market, reduced participation of common shares in the stock exchange, share control concentrated in a few investors, reduced liquidity of traded papers, low disclosure of accounting infor- mation, high volatility of information), hence the need for the adjustments proposed in this study to minimize such limita- tions (Godfrey and Espinosa, 1996; Lessard, 1996; Mariscal and Hargis, 1999; Pereio, 2001; Damodaran, 2002; Assaf Neto, 2014).
The MARR estimate is given by the following expression:

MARR = R (2) Where: MARR minimum attractiveness rate of return Rfg global risk-free rate Rc country risk BCLG country beta BGG industry's average unlevered beta RMG global market return R2 coefficient of determination
The global risk-free rate (Rfg) represents the return on a risk- free investment, in this case the interest rate paid on bonds is- sued by the United States Government Treasury (T-Bond) with a

Indicator|Unit|Result EVA/kg SO2 eq|$ / sulfur dioxide eq|Value added by air acidification EVA/PO4 eq|$ / phosphate equivalent|Value added by aquatic eutrophication EVA/CO2 eq|$ / kg of carbon dioxide eq|Value that contributes to global warming

redemption term of ten years. For country risk (Rc), the Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI) + Brazil is suggested, measured by the JP Morgan bank and based on the risk premium for Brazilian for- eign debt securities in relation to the papers issued by the North American treasury. The country beta (BCLG) is determined by the regression between the local stock market index and the global market index. As a local stock market index, it is suggested to use the daily variation of the IBOVESPA (São Paulo stock exchange in- dex) and, in the case of the global market, the Morgan Stanley Cap- ital International (MSCI); all country world index (ACWI).

This index is calculated by the Morgan Stanley bank and mea- sures the performance of the stock market in 46 countries (23 de- veloped and 23 emerging). As for the unlevered beta of comparable companies in the global market (BGG), it is suggested to deleverage the average beta of comparable companies. The coefficient of deter- mination (R2) is calculated by the regression between the volatil- ity of local market stocks and the variation of country risk (see. Damodaran, 2002; Pereio, 2001; Assaf Neto, 2014; Martinelli et al., 2019; Souza, Gimenes and Binotto, 2019; Cavalheiro, Kremer and Gimenes, 2019).

28: In short, all investments are subject to risk. In order to mini- mize uncertainty and better guide rural producers in decision mak- ing, sensitivity analysis is applied. Sensitivity analysis and Monte Carlo simulation start from the assumption that the behavior of the variables is probabilistic. Therefore, with its application, we seek to analyze how the magnitude of predetermined changes in one or more input variables can influence the value of output variables (Acuña et al., 2018; Talavera et al., 2011).
In this specific case, the variation in EVA/kg is determined in face of variations in endogenous and exogenous variables used in

the preparation of investment cash flow (Coelho Júnior et al., 2008; Casarotto Filho and Kopittke, 2010; Gitman, 2010). In this study, we opt for the sensitivity analysis of EVA/kg due to changes in the following variables: i) net operating revenue, ii) lot operating cost; iii) minimum attractiveness rate of return, and iv) invested capital.

In order to simulate the possible results of the EVA/kg of the investment from probabilistic changes in the magnitude of those previously selected for the sensitivity analysis and already men- tioned at the end of the previous paragraph, the Monte Carlo simu- lation was performed using the software Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS), version 26, developed by the International Business Machines Corporation (IBM).

The Monte Carlo method or simulation explicitly introduces un- certainty in the variables that most affect the EVA/kg results in order to observe its behavior in a risky environment (Martínez- Paz et al., 2014), that is, when using random numbers and proba- bility distributions from a random procedure, a value is generated for each input variable, allowing observing the variability and risk of a proposed investment (Arnold and Yildiz, 2015).

In Monte Carlo simulation applications, triangular and nor- mal distributions are more commonly observed. In a triangular distribution, three parameters are established, namely, the mini- mum, maximum and the mode of the uncertain input; in a nor- mal distribution, it is necessary to know the mean and the stan- dard deviation of the variables whose behavior will be simulated (Lukuyu et al., 2019).

In this study, 10,000 iterations are carried out for the variables considered inputs with uncertainty, the variations of which were suggested by a panel of experts in the sector of activity, which is object of this study, in order to verify the behavior of the output variable, in this case the value of EVA/kg. For each input, a prob- ability distribution and its respective parameters are associated, as shown in (Table S2).

31: From the information shown in Table 1, it was possible to esti- mate the values referring to gas emissions inside and outside the aviary (Table S3). The volume of methane gas emission is equiva- lent between DH and conventional systems. It only increases when there is a greater consumption of feed; therefore, the Griller prod- uct has almost half of the CH4 emissions of Heavy chicken. The ingredients of plant origin have a certain digestibility content: the lower the consumption of these ingredients, the lower the amount of volatile solids found in animal excreta that will be available for decomposition by methanogenic bacteria, which will subsequently release methane gas.

The amount of ammonia from nitrogen in the excreta is greater inside the aviary than outside, when the litter is removed after a few lots and stored. There is at least 50% more ammonia emitted inside the house than outside it. The values are between 0.0016 kg NH3 for Griller products and 0.0047 kg NH3 for organic chickens. Other gases, such as nitric oxide and nitrogen gas, are emitted only after the storage of the litter, when the nitrogen that has not been
G. Martinelli, E. Vogel and M. Decian et al./Sustainable Production and Consumption 24 (2020) 181-193
Table 3 Global warming, acidification and eutrophication for the three production systems.

Impact category|DHa 2400|DHa 2400|DHa 2400|PPb 1200|PPb 1200|PPb 1200|ORG 600 Impact category|Griller|Broiler|Heavy|Griller|Broiler|Heavy|Heavy Global Warming/kg CO2 eq|1.334|1.488|1.550|1.335|1.466|1.556|1.620 Acidification/kg SO2 eq|0.015|0.017|0.018|0.015|0.016|0.018|0.028 Eutrophication/kg PO4 eq|0.029|0.034|0.035|0.029|0.033|0.035|0.044
a DH 2400 Dark house 2400 m² b PP 1200 Positive pressure 1200 m² C ORG 600 - Positive pressure 600 m² organic production.

volatilized inside the aviary is in constant interaction with external environmental factors, such as humidity and aeration. For the soil, there is deposition of phosphorus from the non-metabolization of P available in diets, called phytic phosphorus. For this estimation, values from the Brazilian Tables for Poultry and Swine, recorded by Rostagno et al. (2017), were used based on the standardized ideal digestibility, obtaining an amount of phytic phosphorus for each type of feed and consequently for each type of animal throughout its life cycle.

Another aspect to be considered is the environmental impacts caused by each of the categories analyzed. In the Table 3, the Global Warming impact category shows emissions related to tradi- tional productions, such as DH and PP systems, between 1.33 and 1.55 kg CO2 equivalent per kilogram of live weight. The agricul- tural phase of grain production for feed has the largest contribu- tions of CO2 equivalent. Corn production contributes with almost 9% of emissions to climate change and soybean production turns into bran and contributes with over 6%, with no significant differ- ence between DH and conventional systems in the global warm- ing category because the feed conversions are similar, therefore the amount of grains eaten for each kilogram of meat produced is practically the same. The consumption of electric energy was the second process with the greatest contributions. The largest con- sumption came from the feed factory where all engines are elec- tric. The chickens' housing phase did not show any significance in the total electricity collected, even though it was necessary to op- erate equipment such as feeders, control panels, and aerators.

However, the ORG system showed slightly greater values in all categories of environmental impacts analyzed compared to the tra- ditional system (Table The explanation for these values in rela- tion to the other systems begins from unitary processes chosen at the inventory stage; the agricultural inputs to produce feed were based on the Swiss production of soy and organic corn. The Ecoin- vent library, which made the Swiss inventories of organic inputs available, lists a global mix of fertilizers applied to the organic corn culture, and chicken litter appears as the basis of this type of fer- tilization, being a source of the mineralized nutrients nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P2O5), and potassium (K20).

The inventories use unitary heat generation processes to dry the chicken litter. These processes cause the greatest impacts be- cause their energy matrix is supplied with mineral coal and fos- sil fuels. The finishing ration for the organic product presented 46.7% of the total emissions of CO2 equivalent for one kilogram of chicken. This feed contains in its formulation approximately 73.3% of ground corn, which was also the process of greatest contribution in all types of feed.

36: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Thus, the costs per functional unit produced in conventional poultry houses are close to the costs of DH poultry houses. The decimal difference is greater when compared to the volumes pro- duced annually. When comparing the cost of organic chicken with Griller chicken, there is a significant difference (300%) in the case of the 71-day organic product. The earned costs increase over time so that each lot reaches slaughter weight. There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is

that the management of organic chickens follows its own regula- tion, where there is a minimum requirement of a dark period per day for birds, leading a 600 m² aviary to consume an average of 680 kWh per lot versus an average of 5206 kWh for DH and 2800 kWh for conventional poultry houses. The second reason regards equipment powered by electric energy. The poultry for production and organic chickens follow the standards of PP aviaries, adding only an open area for grazing.

In relation to the revenue related to the production sale, the in- formation is available in Table S5. The average prices paid to pro- ducers range from $ 0.10/bird for Griller products to $ 0.21/bird for Heavy products. Again, there is a singularity for organic chicken. The amount paid by the cooperative to the producer is $ 2.11 per kilogram of live weight. It begins with operating income, which for the analyzed lots comes from the sale of poultry for slaughter and from litter as organic fertilizer. The only deduction is made at the time of sale, where the 2.3% rate, referring to social security contribution tax (FUNRURAL), is applied to the total amount of the invoice.

From these data, it was necessary to determine the operating results of each production system for the period of one year (Table S7), whose values were converted into average dollar rate of Oc- tober 2017 ($ 3.196). Then, the productivity of the invested capital and the operating margin of each production system were deter- mined in order to subsequently calculate the return on investment (ROI). Productivity relates revenues to the total value of the capital invested, identifying the number of times (turnover) this capital is realized in the year.

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

The results in (Table S6) evidence that the EVA is positive, that is, it creates economic value for investors in all production systems, except for the PP 1200 system (Heavy), whose EVA is equal to -$ 235.68.
Regarding the added value for the analyzed categories, the re- sults can be identified in Fig. 2.

In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities

The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. Korin carried out the first export of organic chickens from Brazil and other countries have already shown interest in buying their chickens, such as Peru, Colombia, Chile, Angola, United Arab Emirates, and some European
c)

countries, these in the study period. Korin grew 380% in the last 7 years and, in 2016, started its sales in the international mar- ket, exporting to Hong Kong, Europe, and the United Arab Emirates (Korin, 2020).

According to producers' association it is estimated that the vol- ume of organic production in Brazil is circa of 300 mil t/year, which generates a market of US$200 million/year. Most of the Brazilian organic production (80%) is in the South and Southeast region. Around 85% of production is exported, mainly to Europe, the United States, and Japan. The remaining (15%) is distributed on the domestic market (CPT, 2020). On the website of the Arab- Brazilian News Agency is available some information on the ex- port of organic chicken from Brazil to the United Arab Emirates (AnBA, 2020).

The organic food consumption in Brazil grows at a rate of 25% per year, with 17,000 certified properties in all the federa- tion units; 63% are exclusive organic producers that use around one million hectares. In 2018, organic products' consumption in Brazil was estimated at $1,029,866.12 (to the average dollar of $3,884), which requires regulation to assure consumers that they are purchasing an organic product, that is, a product that com- plies with legal regulations (Law No. 10,831 / 2003 and Decree No. 6,323 / 2007), established for organic agriculture in the country (Sebrae, 2020).
a) Operating Costs (OC): the ORG 600 system has the lowest Operating Cost ($13,430.47) when compared to the other production systems. The OC of the DH 2400 Broiler system,
i.e., is 2.8 times greater than the operating cost of the ORG 600 ($37,551.80/$13,430.47), see Table S7.

<list>b) NOPAT: the NOPAT value is produced by the difference be- tween NOR, OC, and taxes paid by the producer, therefore it identifies the profitability of the production system. In Ta- ble S7 can be seen that NOPAT obtained in the ORG 600 is 3.36 times greater than that obtained in the DH 2400 Broiler system ($80,072.58/$23,848.15), that is, the NOPAT magni- tude of the ORG 600 system is derived from a higher NOR and lower OC compared to the values identified in the DH 2400 Broiler system (items a and b, described above). 
c) Invested Capital (IC): the amount of Invested Capital in the ORG 600 system is much lower than that invested in other production systems. The DH 2400 Broiler system, for in- stance, is found to demand 2.35 times more IC than the ORG 600 system ($226,465.98/$96,519.65), see Table S7. 
d) Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): ROIC is a rate assem- bled by the relationship between NOPAT and invested cap- ital. Among the production systems analyzed the ORG 600 showed the highest NOPAT and the lowest capital invested, thus justifying the value of its ROIC (82.96%) significantly higher than the other rates of return, see Table S7. 
e) Cost of Invested Capital (CIC): the CIC depends on the amount of resources invested in the production system and on the attractiveness of the investment to the producer, called MARR. In this study, the MARR was determined at 6.75% (see Table S6), therefore, given the amount of IC and MARR, it is noted that the ORG 600 system had the lowest CIC ($65.511 X 0.0675 = $4,421.99)</list>

Based on the explanations given in the previous items, the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system is justified. The mag- nitude of its value ($1.123/kg) concerning the other systems is de- termined by the higher sale price of the product (24.75 and 4.53 times higher for the producer and the consumer, respectively), the lower operating cost ($13,430.47) and the lower demand for in- vested capital ($96,519.65). The higher price and lower cost in- crease profitability ($80,072.58). Higher profit with less demand for invested capital produces a higher rate of return (82.96%). A higher rate of return with a lower cost of capital increases the value of EVA/kg ($1.123).

For aquatic eutrophication, the results are similar to those for acidification (Fig. 2-c). The products that most affected this cate- gory were Heavy chickens, with emission values of 0.04 kg PO4 eq. per functional unit and with positive EVA values for DH aviaries and negative for conventional aviaries. In relation to the category global warming (100 yr) (Fig. 2-a) for DH systems and for conven- tional systems, the values were established between 1.3 kg CO2 eq and 1.6 kg CO2 eq. The amount of equivalent gases emitted in or- ganic systems by UF is almost the same as in the systems in com- parison, not exceeding 2%, that is 1.62 kg CO2 eq. for an average of 1.55 kg CO2 eq per UF. The EVA that the organic product generates represents approximately 80 times the EVA of the Broiler product in DH systems.

When results are normalized for each $ 1.00 EVA, it is possi- ble to note that organic systems are the lowest emitters regarding the global warming (100 yr) impact category (Table S8). The re- sults show that for each $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system emits 1,44 kg CO2 eq. These values very close to the PP 1200 Heavy and DH 2400 broiler systems. The difference is the number of FUs that each system needs to generate this economic value. In the second column of the same table, it is observed that to generate $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system needs only one FU, whereas the DH 2400 Griller and DH 2400 Broiler systems need 83 and 71 FUs, respec- tively.
37: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Thus, the costs per functional unit produced in conventional poultry houses are close to the costs of DH poultry houses. The decimal difference is greater when compared to the volumes pro- duced annually. When comparing the cost of organic chicken with Griller chicken, there is a significant difference (300%) in the case of the 71-day organic product. The earned costs increase over time so that each lot reaches slaughter weight. There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is
Table 4 Unit costs.

|DHa 2400|DHa 2400|DHa 2400|ppb 1200|ppb 1200|ppb 1200|"ORG 600 Organic" |Griller|Broiler|Heavy|Griller|Broiler|Heavy|"ORG 600 Organic" Final lodging (birds/lot)|46,146|33,469|31,968|20,095|14,400|13,440|4,560 Average slaughter weight (kg)|1.50|2.90|3.50|1.50|2.90|3.50|3.30 Total cost ($)|4,993.09|5,816.26|5,962.09|2,546.25|2,927.80|3,174.17|3,213.50 Cost per chicken delivered ($)|0.11|0.18|0.19|0.13|0.20|0.23|0.70 Cost per kg of chicken delivered ($)|0.07|0.06|0.05|0.08|0.07|0.07|0.21

that the management of organic chickens follows its own regula- tion, where there is a minimum requirement of a dark period per day for birds, leading a 600 m² aviary to consume an average of 680 kWh per lot versus an average of 5206 kWh for DH and 2800 kWh for conventional poultry houses. The second reason regards equipment powered by electric energy. The poultry for production and organic chickens follow the standards of PP aviaries, adding only an open area for grazing.

In relation to the revenue related to the production sale, the in- formation is available in Table S5. The average prices paid to pro- ducers range from $ 0.10/bird for Griller products to $ 0.21/bird for Heavy products. Again, there is a singularity for organic chicken. The amount paid by the cooperative to the producer is $ 2.11 per kilogram of live weight. It begins with operating income, which for the analyzed lots comes from the sale of poultry for slaughter and from litter as organic fertilizer. The only deduction is made at the time of sale, where the 2.3% rate, referring to social security contribution tax (FUNRURAL), is applied to the total amount of the invoice.

From these data, it was necessary to determine the operating results of each production system for the period of one year (Table S7), whose values were converted into average dollar rate of Oc- tober 2017 ($ 3.196). Then, the productivity of the invested capital and the operating margin of each production system were deter- mined in order to subsequently calculate the return on investment (ROI). Productivity relates revenues to the total value of the capital invested, identifying the number of times (turnover) this capital is realized in the year.

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

The results in (Table S6) evidence that the EVA is positive, that is, it creates economic value for investors in all production systems, except for the PP 1200 system (Heavy), whose EVA is equal to -$ 235.68.
Regarding the added value for the analyzed categories, the re- sults can be identified in Fig. 2.

In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities

The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities
