The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. Korin carried out the first export of organic chickens from Brazil and other countries have already shown interest in buying their chickens, such as Peru, Colombia, Chile, Angola, United Arab Emirates, and some European
c)

countries, these in the study period. Korin grew 380% in the last 7 years and, in 2016, started its sales in the international mar- ket, exporting to Hong Kong, Europe, and the United Arab Emirates (Korin, 2020).

According to producers' association it is estimated that the vol- ume of organic production in Brazil is circa of 300 mil t/year, which generates a market of US$200 million/year. Most of the Brazilian organic production (80%) is in the South and Southeast region. Around 85% of production is exported, mainly to Europe, the United States, and Japan. The remaining (15%) is distributed on the domestic market (CPT, 2020). On the website of the Arab- Brazilian News Agency is available some information on the ex- port of organic chicken from Brazil to the United Arab Emirates (AnBA, 2020).

The organic food consumption in Brazil grows at a rate of 25% per year, with 17,000 certified properties in all the federa- tion units; 63% are exclusive organic producers that use around one million hectares. In 2018, organic products' consumption in Brazil was estimated at $1,029,866.12 (to the average dollar of $3,884), which requires regulation to assure consumers that they are purchasing an organic product, that is, a product that com- plies with legal regulations (Law No. 10,831 / 2003 and Decree No. 6,323 / 2007), established for organic agriculture in the country (Sebrae, 2020).
a) Operating Costs (OC): the ORG 600 system has the lowest Operating Cost ($13,430.47) when compared to the other production systems. The OC of the DH 2400 Broiler system,
i.e., is 2.8 times greater than the operating cost of the ORG 600 ($37,551.80/$13,430.47), see Table S7.

<list>b) NOPAT: the NOPAT value is produced by the difference be- tween NOR, OC, and taxes paid by the producer, therefore it identifies the profitability of the production system. In Ta- ble S7 can be seen that NOPAT obtained in the ORG 600 is 3.36 times greater than that obtained in the DH 2400 Broiler system ($80,072.58/$23,848.15), that is, the NOPAT magni- tude of the ORG 600 system is derived from a higher NOR and lower OC compared to the values identified in the DH 2400 Broiler system (items a and b, described above). 
c) Invested Capital (IC): the amount of Invested Capital in the ORG 600 system is much lower than that invested in other production systems. The DH 2400 Broiler system, for in- stance, is found to demand 2.35 times more IC than the ORG 600 system ($226,465.98/$96,519.65), see Table S7. 
d) Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): ROIC is a rate assem- bled by the relationship between NOPAT and invested cap- ital. Among the production systems analyzed the ORG 600 showed the highest NOPAT and the lowest capital invested, thus justifying the value of its ROIC (82.96%) significantly higher than the other rates of return, see Table S7. 
e) Cost of Invested Capital (CIC): the CIC depends on the amount of resources invested in the production system and on the attractiveness of the investment to the producer, called MARR. In this study, the MARR was determined at 6.75% (see Table S6), therefore, given the amount of IC and MARR, it is noted that the ORG 600 system had the lowest CIC ($65.511 X 0.0675 = $4,421.99)</list>

Based on the explanations given in the previous items, the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system is justified. The mag- nitude of its value ($1.123/kg) concerning the other systems is de- termined by the higher sale price of the product (24.75 and 4.53 times higher for the producer and the consumer, respectively), the lower operating cost ($13,430.47) and the lower demand for in- vested capital ($96,519.65). The higher price and lower cost in- crease profitability ($80,072.58). Higher profit with less demand for invested capital produces a higher rate of return (82.96%). A higher rate of return with a lower cost of capital increases the value of EVA/kg ($1.123).

For aquatic eutrophication, the results are similar to those for acidification (Fig. 2-c). The products that most affected this cate- gory were Heavy chickens, with emission values of 0.04 kg PO4 eq. per functional unit and with positive EVA values for DH aviaries and negative for conventional aviaries. In relation to the category global warming (100 yr) (Fig. 2-a) for DH systems and for conven- tional systems, the values were established between 1.3 kg CO2 eq and 1.6 kg CO2 eq. The amount of equivalent gases emitted in or- ganic systems by UF is almost the same as in the systems in com- parison, not exceeding 2%, that is 1.62 kg CO2 eq. for an average of 1.55 kg CO2 eq per UF. The EVA that the organic product generates represents approximately 80 times the EVA of the Broiler product in DH systems.

When results are normalized for each $ 1.00 EVA, it is possi- ble to note that organic systems are the lowest emitters regarding the global warming (100 yr) impact category (Table S8). The re- sults show that for each $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system emits 1,44 kg CO2 eq. These values very close to the PP 1200 Heavy and DH 2400 broiler systems. The difference is the number of FUs that each system needs to generate this economic value. In the second column of the same table, it is observed that to generate $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system needs only one FU, whereas the DH 2400 Griller and DH 2400 Broiler systems need 83 and 71 FUs, respec- tively.
39: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Thus, the costs per functional unit produced in conventional poultry houses are close to the costs of DH poultry houses. The decimal difference is greater when compared to the volumes pro- duced annually. When comparing the cost of organic chicken with Griller chicken, there is a significant difference (300%) in the case of the 71-day organic product. The earned costs increase over time so that each lot reaches slaughter weight. There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is

that the management of organic chickens follows its own regula- tion, where there is a minimum requirement of a dark period per day for birds, leading a 600 m² aviary to consume an average of 680 kWh per lot versus an average of 5206 kWh for DH and 2800 kWh for conventional poultry houses. The second reason regards equipment powered by electric energy. The poultry for production and organic chickens follow the standards of PP aviaries, adding only an open area for grazing.

In relation to the revenue related to the production sale, the in- formation is available in Table S5. The average prices paid to pro- ducers range from $ 0.10/bird for Griller products to $ 0.21/bird for Heavy products. Again, there is a singularity for organic chicken. The amount paid by the cooperative to the producer is $ 2.11 per kilogram of live weight. It begins with operating income, which for the analyzed lots comes from the sale of poultry for slaughter and from litter as organic fertilizer. The only deduction is made at the time of sale, where the 2.3% rate, referring to social security contribution tax (FUNRURAL), is applied to the total amount of the invoice.

From these data, it was necessary to determine the operating results of each production system for the period of one year (Table S7), whose values were converted into average dollar rate of Oc- tober 2017 ($ 3.196). Then, the productivity of the invested capital and the operating margin of each production system were deter- mined in order to subsequently calculate the return on investment (ROI). Productivity relates revenues to the total value of the capital invested, identifying the number of times (turnover) this capital is realized in the year.

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is
Table 4 Unit costs.

that the management of organic chickens follows its own regula- tion, where there is a minimum requirement of a dark period per day for birds, leading a 600 m² aviary to consume an average of 680 kWh per lot versus an average of 5206 kWh for DH and 2800 kWh for conventional poultry houses. The second reason regards equipment powered by electric energy. The poultry for production and organic chickens follow the standards of PP aviaries, adding only an open area for grazing.

In relation to the revenue related to the production sale, the in- formation is available in Table S5. The average prices paid to pro- ducers range from $ 0.10/bird for Griller products to $ 0.21/bird for Heavy products. Again, there is a singularity for organic chicken. The amount paid by the cooperative to the producer is $ 2.11 per kilogram of live weight. It begins with operating income, which for the analyzed lots comes from the sale of poultry for slaughter and from litter as organic fertilizer. The only deduction is made at the time of sale, where the 2.3% rate, referring to social security contribution tax (FUNRURAL), is applied to the total amount of the invoice.

From these data, it was necessary to determine the operating results of each production system for the period of one year (Table S7), whose values were converted into average dollar rate of Oc- tober 2017 ($ 3.196). Then, the productivity of the invested capital and the operating margin of each production system were deter- mined in order to subsequently calculate the return on investment (ROI). Productivity relates revenues to the total value of the capital invested, identifying the number of times (turnover) this capital is realized in the year.

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

The results in (Table S6) evidence that the EVA is positive, that is, it creates economic value for investors in all production systems, except for the PP 1200 system (Heavy), whose EVA is equal to -$ 235.68.
Regarding the added value for the analyzed categories, the re- sults can be identified in Fig. 2.

In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities

The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. Korin carried out the first export of organic chickens from Brazil and other countries have already shown interest in buying their chickens, such as Peru, Colombia, Chile, Angola, United Arab Emirates, and some European
c)

countries, these in the study period. Korin grew 380% in the last 7 years and, in 2016, started its sales in the international mar- ket, exporting to Hong Kong, Europe, and the United Arab Emirates (Korin, 2020).

According to producers' association it is estimated that the vol- ume of organic production in Brazil is circa of 300 mil t/year, which generates a market of US$200 million/year. Most of the Brazilian organic production (80%) is in the South and Southeast region. Around 85% of production is exported, mainly to Europe, the United States, and Japan. The remaining (15%) is distributed on the domestic market (CPT, 2020). On the website of the Arab- Brazilian News Agency is available some information on the ex- port of organic chicken from Brazil to the United Arab Emirates (AnBA, 2020).

The organic food consumption in Brazil grows at a rate of 25% per year, with 17,000 certified properties in all the federa- tion units; 63% are exclusive organic producers that use around one million hectares. In 2018, organic products' consumption in Brazil was estimated at $1,029,866.12 (to the average dollar of $3,884), which requires regulation to assure consumers that they are purchasing an organic product, that is, a product that com- plies with legal regulations (Law No. 10,831 / 2003 and Decree No. 6,323 / 2007), established for organic agriculture in the country (Sebrae, 2020).
a) Operating Costs (OC): the ORG 600 system has the lowest Operating Cost ($13,430.47) when compared to the other production systems. The OC of the DH 2400 Broiler system,
i.e., is 2.8 times greater than the operating cost of the ORG 600 ($37,551.80/$13,430.47), see Table S7.

<list>b) NOPAT: the NOPAT value is produced by the difference be- tween NOR, OC, and taxes paid by the producer, therefore it identifies the profitability of the production system. In Ta- ble S7 can be seen that NOPAT obtained in the ORG 600 is 3.36 times greater than that obtained in the DH 2400 Broiler system ($80,072.58/$23,848.15), that is, the NOPAT magni- tude of the ORG 600 system is derived from a higher NOR and lower OC compared to the values identified in the DH 2400 Broiler system (items a and b, described above). 
c) Invested Capital (IC): the amount of Invested Capital in the ORG 600 system is much lower than that invested in other production systems. The DH 2400 Broiler system, for in- stance, is found to demand 2.35 times more IC than the ORG 600 system ($226,465.98/$96,519.65), see Table S7. 
d) Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): ROIC is a rate assem- bled by the relationship between NOPAT and invested cap- ital. Among the production systems analyzed the ORG 600 showed the highest NOPAT and the lowest capital invested, thus justifying the value of its ROIC (82.96%) significantly higher than the other rates of return, see Table S7. 
e) Cost of Invested Capital (CIC): the CIC depends on the amount of resources invested in the production system and on the attractiveness of the investment to the producer, called MARR. In this study, the MARR was determined at 6.75% (see Table S6), therefore, given the amount of IC and MARR, it is noted that the ORG 600 system had the lowest CIC ($65.511 X 0.0675 = $4,421.99)</list>

Based on the explanations given in the previous items, the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system is justified. The mag- nitude of its value ($1.123/kg) concerning the other systems is de- termined by the higher sale price of the product (24.75 and 4.53 times higher for the producer and the consumer, respectively), the lower operating cost ($13,430.47) and the lower demand for in- vested capital ($96,519.65). The higher price and lower cost in- crease profitability ($80,072.58). Higher profit with less demand for invested capital produces a higher rate of return (82.96%). A higher rate of return with a lower cost of capital increases the value of EVA/kg ($1.123).

For aquatic eutrophication, the results are similar to those for acidification (Fig. 2-c). The products that most affected this cate- gory were Heavy chickens, with emission values of 0.04 kg PO4 eq. per functional unit and with positive EVA values for DH aviaries and negative for conventional aviaries. In relation to the category global warming (100 yr) (Fig. 2-a) for DH systems and for conven- tional systems, the values were established between 1.3 kg CO2 eq and 1.6 kg CO2 eq. The amount of equivalent gases emitted in or- ganic systems by UF is almost the same as in the systems in com- parison, not exceeding 2%, that is 1.62 kg CO2 eq. for an average of 1.55 kg CO2 eq per UF. The EVA that the organic product generates represents approximately 80 times the EVA of the Broiler product in DH systems.

When results are normalized for each $ 1.00 EVA, it is possi- ble to note that organic systems are the lowest emitters regarding the global warming (100 yr) impact category (Table S8). The re- sults show that for each $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system emits 1,44 kg CO2 eq. These values very close to the PP 1200 Heavy and DH 2400 broiler systems. The difference is the number of FUs that each system needs to generate this economic value. In the second column of the same table, it is observed that to generate $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system needs only one FU, whereas the DH 2400 Griller and DH 2400 Broiler systems need 83 and 71 FUs, respec- tively.
41: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Based on the explanations given in the previous items, the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system is justified. The mag- nitude of its value ($1.123/kg) concerning the other systems is de- termined by the higher sale price of the product (24.75 and 4.53 times higher for the producer and the consumer, respectively), the lower operating cost ($13,430.47) and the lower demand for in- vested capital ($96,519.65). The higher price and lower cost in- crease profitability ($80,072.58). Higher profit with less demand for invested capital produces a higher rate of return (82.96%). A higher rate of return with a lower cost of capital increases the value of EVA/kg ($1.123).

For aquatic eutrophication, the results are similar to those for acidification (Fig. 2-c). The products that most affected this cate- gory were Heavy chickens, with emission values of 0.04 kg PO4 eq. per functional unit and with positive EVA values for DH aviaries and negative for conventional aviaries. In relation to the category global warming (100 yr) (Fig. 2-a) for DH systems and for conven- tional systems, the values were established between 1.3 kg CO2 eq and 1.6 kg CO2 eq. The amount of equivalent gases emitted in or- ganic systems by UF is almost the same as in the systems in com- parison, not exceeding 2%, that is 1.62 kg CO2 eq. for an average of 1.55 kg CO2 eq per UF. The EVA that the organic product generates represents approximately 80 times the EVA of the Broiler product in DH systems.

When results are normalized for each $ 1.00 EVA, it is possi- ble to note that organic systems are the lowest emitters regarding the global warming (100 yr) impact category (Table S8). The re- sults show that for each $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system emits 1,44 kg CO2 eq. These values very close to the PP 1200 Heavy and DH 2400 broiler systems. The difference is the number of FUs that each system needs to generate this economic value. In the second column of the same table, it is observed that to generate $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system needs only one FU, whereas the DH 2400 Griller and DH 2400 Broiler systems need 83 and 71 FUs, respec- tively.
42: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Thus, the costs per functional unit produced in conventional poultry houses are close to the costs of DH poultry houses. The decimal difference is greater when compared to the volumes pro- duced annually. When comparing the cost of organic chicken with Griller chicken, there is a significant difference (300%) in the case of the 71-day organic product. The earned costs increase over time so that each lot reaches slaughter weight. There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is

that the management of organic chickens follows its own regula- tion, where there is a minimum requirement of a dark period per day for birds, leading a 600 m² aviary to consume an average of 680 kWh per lot versus an average of 5206 kWh for DH and 2800 kWh for conventional poultry houses. The second reason regards equipment powered by electric energy. The poultry for production and organic chickens follow the standards of PP aviaries, adding only an open area for grazing.

In relation to the revenue related to the production sale, the in- formation is available in Table S5. The average prices paid to pro- ducers range from $ 0.10/bird for Griller products to $ 0.21/bird for Heavy products. Again, there is a singularity for organic chicken. The amount paid by the cooperative to the producer is $ 2.11 per kilogram of live weight. It begins with operating income, which for the analyzed lots comes from the sale of poultry for slaughter and from litter as organic fertilizer. The only deduction is made at the time of sale, where the 2.3% rate, referring to social security contribution tax (FUNRURAL), is applied to the total amount of the invoice.

From these data, it was necessary to determine the operating results of each production system for the period of one year (Table S7), whose values were converted into average dollar rate of Oc- tober 2017 ($ 3.196). Then, the productivity of the invested capital and the operating margin of each production system were deter- mined in order to subsequently calculate the return on investment (ROI). Productivity relates revenues to the total value of the capital invested, identifying the number of times (turnover) this capital is realized in the year.

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

The results in (Table S6) evidence that the EVA is positive, that is, it creates economic value for investors in all production systems, except for the PP 1200 system (Heavy), whose EVA is equal to -$ 235.68.
Regarding the added value for the analyzed categories, the re- sults can be identified in Fig. 2.

In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities

The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. Korin carried out the first export of organic chickens from Brazil and other countries have already shown interest in buying their chickens, such as Peru, Colombia, Chile, Angola, United Arab Emirates, and some European
c)

countries, these in the study period. Korin grew 380% in the last 7 years and, in 2016, started its sales in the international mar- ket, exporting to Hong Kong, Europe, and the United Arab Emirates (Korin, 2020).

According to producers' association it is estimated that the vol- ume of organic production in Brazil is circa of 300 mil t/year, which generates a market of US$200 million/year. Most of the Brazilian organic production (80%) is in the South and Southeast region. Around 85% of production is exported, mainly to Europe, the United States, and Japan. The remaining (15%) is distributed on the domestic market (CPT, 2020). On the website of the Arab- Brazilian News Agency is available some information on the ex- port of organic chicken from Brazil to the United Arab Emirates (AnBA, 2020).

The organic food consumption in Brazil grows at a rate of 25% per year, with 17,000 certified properties in all the federa- tion units; 63% are exclusive organic producers that use around one million hectares. In 2018, organic products' consumption in Brazil was estimated at $1,029,866.12 (to the average dollar of $3,884), which requires regulation to assure consumers that they are purchasing an organic product, that is, a product that com- plies with legal regulations (Law No. 10,831 / 2003 and Decree No. 6,323 / 2007), established for organic agriculture in the country (Sebrae, 2020).
a) Operating Costs (OC): the ORG 600 system has the lowest Operating Cost ($13,430.47) when compared to the other production systems. The OC of the DH 2400 Broiler system,
i.e., is 2.8 times greater than the operating cost of the ORG 600 ($37,551.80/$13,430.47), see Table S7.

In Ta- ble S7 can be seen that NOPAT obtained in the ORG 600 is 3.36 times greater than that obtained in the DH 2400 Broiler system ($80,072.58/$23,848.15), that is, the NOPAT magni- tude of the ORG 600 system is derived from a higher NOR and lower OC compared to the values identified in the DH 2400 Broiler system (items a and b, described above). 
c) Invested Capital (IC): the amount of Invested Capital in the ORG 600 system is much lower than that invested in other production systems. The DH 2400 Broiler system, for in- stance, is found to demand 2.35 times more IC than the ORG 600 system ($226,465.98/$96,519.65), see Table S7. 
d) Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): ROIC is a rate assem- bled by the relationship between NOPAT and invested cap- ital. Among the production systems analyzed the ORG 600 showed the highest NOPAT and the lowest capital invested, thus justifying the value of its ROIC (82.96%) significantly higher than the other rates of return, see Table S7.

The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. Korin carried out the first export of organic chickens from Brazil and other countries have already shown interest in buying their chickens, such as Peru, Colombia, Chile, Angola, United Arab Emirates, and some European
c)

countries, these in the study period. Korin grew 380% in the last 7 years and, in 2016, started its sales in the international mar- ket, exporting to Hong Kong, Europe, and the United Arab Emirates (Korin, 2020).

According to producers' association it is estimated that the vol- ume of organic production in Brazil is circa of 300 mil t/year, which generates a market of US$200 million/year. Most of the Brazilian organic production (80%) is in the South and Southeast region. Around 85% of production is exported, mainly to Europe, the United States, and Japan. The remaining (15%) is distributed on the domestic market (CPT, 2020). On the website of the Arab- Brazilian News Agency is available some information on the ex- port of organic chicken from Brazil to the United Arab Emirates (AnBA, 2020).

The organic food consumption in Brazil grows at a rate of 25% per year, with 17,000 certified properties in all the federa- tion units; 63% are exclusive organic producers that use around one million hectares. In 2018, organic products' consumption in Brazil was estimated at $1,029,866.12 (to the average dollar of $3,884), which requires regulation to assure consumers that they are purchasing an organic product, that is, a product that com- plies with legal regulations (Law No. 10,831 / 2003 and Decree No. 6,323 / 2007), established for organic agriculture in the country (Sebrae, 2020).
a) Operating Costs (OC): the ORG 600 system has the lowest Operating Cost ($13,430.47) when compared to the other production systems. The OC of the DH 2400 Broiler system,
i.e., is 2.8 times greater than the operating cost of the ORG 600 ($37,551.80/$13,430.47), see Table S7.

<list>b) NOPAT: the NOPAT value is produced by the difference be- tween NOR, OC, and taxes paid by the producer, therefore it identifies the profitability of the production system. In Ta- ble S7 can be seen that NOPAT obtained in the ORG 600 is 3.36 times greater than that obtained in the DH 2400 Broiler system ($80,072.58/$23,848.15), that is, the NOPAT magni- tude of the ORG 600 system is derived from a higher NOR and lower OC compared to the values identified in the DH 2400 Broiler system (items a and b, described above). 
c) Invested Capital (IC): the amount of Invested Capital in the ORG 600 system is much lower than that invested in other production systems. The DH 2400 Broiler system, for in- stance, is found to demand 2.35 times more IC than the ORG 600 system ($226,465.98/$96,519.65), see Table S7. 
d) Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): ROIC is a rate assem- bled by the relationship between NOPAT and invested cap- ital. Among the production systems analyzed the ORG 600 showed the highest NOPAT and the lowest capital invested, thus justifying the value of its ROIC (82.96%) significantly higher than the other rates of return, see Table S7. 
e) Cost of Invested Capital (CIC): the CIC depends on the amount of resources invested in the production system and on the attractiveness of the investment to the producer, called MARR. In this study, the MARR was determined at 6.75% (see Table S6), therefore, given the amount of IC and MARR, it is noted that the ORG 600 system had the lowest CIC ($65.511 X 0.0675 = $4,421.99)</list>

Based on the explanations given in the previous items, the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system is justified. The mag- nitude of its value ($1.123/kg) concerning the other systems is de- termined by the higher sale price of the product (24.75 and 4.53 times higher for the producer and the consumer, respectively), the lower operating cost ($13,430.47) and the lower demand for in- vested capital ($96,519.65). The higher price and lower cost in- crease profitability ($80,072.58). Higher profit with less demand for invested capital produces a higher rate of return (82.96%). A higher rate of return with a lower cost of capital increases the value of EVA/kg ($1.123).

For aquatic eutrophication, the results are similar to those for acidification (Fig. 2-c). The products that most affected this cate- gory were Heavy chickens, with emission values of 0.04 kg PO4 eq. per functional unit and with positive EVA values for DH aviaries and negative for conventional aviaries. In relation to the category global warming (100 yr) (Fig. 2-a) for DH systems and for conven- tional systems, the values were established between 1.3 kg CO2 eq and 1.6 kg CO2 eq. The amount of equivalent gases emitted in or- ganic systems by UF is almost the same as in the systems in com- parison, not exceeding 2%, that is 1.62 kg CO2 eq. for an average of 1.55 kg CO2 eq per UF. The EVA that the organic product generates represents approximately 80 times the EVA of the Broiler product in DH systems.

When results are normalized for each $ 1.00 EVA, it is possi- ble to note that organic systems are the lowest emitters regarding the global warming (100 yr) impact category (Table S8). The re- sults show that for each $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system emits 1,44 kg CO2 eq. These values very close to the PP 1200 Heavy and DH 2400 broiler systems. The difference is the number of FUs that each system needs to generate this economic value. In the second column of the same table, it is observed that to generate $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system needs only one FU, whereas the DH 2400 Griller and DH 2400 Broiler systems need 83 and 71 FUs, respec- tively.
44: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Thus, the costs per functional unit produced in conventional poultry houses are close to the costs of DH poultry houses. The decimal difference is greater when compared to the volumes pro- duced annually. When comparing the cost of organic chicken with Griller chicken, there is a significant difference (300%) in the case of the 71-day organic product. The earned costs increase over time so that each lot reaches slaughter weight. There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is

that the management of organic chickens follows its own regula- tion, where there is a minimum requirement of a dark period per day for birds, leading a 600 m² aviary to consume an average of 680 kWh per lot versus an average of 5206 kWh for DH and 2800 kWh for conventional poultry houses. The second reason regards equipment powered by electric energy. The poultry for production and organic chickens follow the standards of PP aviaries, adding only an open area for grazing.

In relation to the revenue related to the production sale, the in- formation is available in Table S5. The average prices paid to pro- ducers range from $ 0.10/bird for Griller products to $ 0.21/bird for Heavy products. Again, there is a singularity for organic chicken. The amount paid by the cooperative to the producer is $ 2.11 per kilogram of live weight. It begins with operating income, which for the analyzed lots comes from the sale of poultry for slaughter and from litter as organic fertilizer. The only deduction is made at the time of sale, where the 2.3% rate, referring to social security contribution tax (FUNRURAL), is applied to the total amount of the invoice.

From these data, it was necessary to determine the operating results of each production system for the period of one year (Table S7), whose values were converted into average dollar rate of Oc- tober 2017 ($ 3.196). Then, the productivity of the invested capital and the operating margin of each production system were deter- mined in order to subsequently calculate the return on investment (ROI). Productivity relates revenues to the total value of the capital invested, identifying the number of times (turnover) this capital is realized in the year.

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

The results in (Table S6) evidence that the EVA is positive, that is, it creates economic value for investors in all production systems, except for the PP 1200 system (Heavy), whose EVA is equal to -$ 235.68.
Regarding the added value for the analyzed categories, the re- sults can be identified in Fig. 2.

In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities

The 2.75% rate was applied to provision the income tax and to calculate the NOPAT. The operating margin, given by the relation between NOPAT and total annual revenue, was 24-37% for conven- tional and DH systems, respectively.

The following information was used to determine the MARR us- ing the AH-CAPM model: a) global risk-free rate (Rfg: interest rate paid on bonds issued by the United States Government Treasury with a 30-year redemption term obtained on 7/31/2019, https:// www.treasury.gov); b) country risk (Rc: JP Morgan's EMBI + Brazil index obtained on 7/31/2019, http://www.ipeadata.gov.br) c) re- turn on the global market (RMG): annual average of the Morgan Stanley Capital International MSCI ACWI index for the period from 01/01/2004 to 07/31/2019, http://msci.com); d) country beta (BCLG: regression between the daily variation of the IBOVESPA index from 01/10/2016 to 07/31/2019 and the daily variation of the MSCI ACWI index in the same period); d) unlevered beta (BGG): the average unlevered beta of the companies JBS SA and BRF SA was used as a proxy for the sector's risk. The beta was unlevered by the BNA = {BA/[1 + (P)]}} model, being BNA the non-levered beta, BA the levered beta of each of the companies obtained by the regression between the daily variations of the IBOVESPA index and the daily variations of the share prices of the respective com- panies in B3 from 08/01/2016 to 07/31/2019 (http://investing.com), t corresponds to the Income Tax and Social Contribution rate, D are

the company's debts, and E is the value of net worth; f) coefficient of determination (R2): regression between the IBOVESPA index and the EMBI + Brazil index from 10/01/2016 to 7/31/2019. The values calculated for the variables used in the AH-CAPM model are shown in (Table S6).

The results in (Table S6) evidence that the EVA is positive, that is, it creates economic value for investors in all production systems, except for the PP 1200 system (Heavy), whose EVA is equal to -$ 235.68.
Regarding the added value for the analyzed categories, the re- sults can be identified in Fig. 2.

In acidification, negative pressure DH systems and conventional systems show similarities to the acidification category, but there is a disparity in the economic vector (Fig. 2-b). Each kilogram of chicken produced in DH systems generates approximately 0.02 kg SO2 eq. For the Broiler product, the ratio of this system is 1:1. In conventional production systems, emissions are also close to 0.02 kg SO2 eq. However, values have shown destruction of economic value for all products. The negative highlight of this system is the production of Heavy chickens, which remained at the average of the emissions of the same product produced in the DH aviary but presented a negative economic result of $ -0.001. None of the sys- tems presented an economic or environmental performance similar as the ORG system. The emissions are 0.028 kg SO2 eq., but with an economic gain of $ 1.12 per FU. Thus, as the comparisons with systems that produce organic chicken were also represented graph- ically (see Fig. 2), despite the ORG system presenting the highest EVA when compared to the other systems.

For the sake of example, we made a comparative analysis with the DH 2400 Broiler production system. This was done to justify the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system. The result depends on the values calculated for the following elements of revenues and costs.:

a) Net Operating Revenue (NOR): Net Operating Revenue is com- pound by the multiplication of the quantity sold (lots or kg) at the unit sale price of the product, deducting the taxes paid by the producer. When comparing the ROL obtained in the ORG 600 with that of DH 2400 Broiler system, it is noted that the ORG 600 system ROL is 1.52 times higher ($96,211.14/$63,178.26), as shown in Table S7.

Although the production obtained in the DH 2400 system is 9.57 times greater than that obtained in the ORG 600 system (626.661kg/65.511kg), see Table S7, the selling price per bird ob- tained in the ORG 600 system is 24.75 times higher ($4.95/$0.20), see Table S5. The price received per bird in the ORG 600 system was gathering directly in an ecological association producers lo- cated in the South Region of Brazil, specifically in the state of Santa Catarina (Santa Rosa de Lima city), consisted by 50 families of eco- logical farmers, all smallholders, whose mission is to unveil actions in the area of ecological agriculture and solidary popular economy, aiming to build sustainable rural development for the communities

The retail organic chicken price paid by the consumer, on aver- age, is equal to $6.07/kg (Will Orgânicos, 2020), by the exchange of the data collection date for October 2017 ($3.196). On the other hand, the chicken price produced in conventional systems, on av- erage, is equal to $1.34/kg (Notícias Agrícolas, 2020). Thereby, the price paid by the consumer in kg of organic chicken is 4.53 times higher than the price paid for the chicken produced in conven- tional systems, specifically in the DH 2400 Broiler system. How- ever, although the organic chicken sale price is significantly higher, it is noteworthy that the market share of this product in the Brazil- ian market is lower than that of other products, given the income inequality, the low per capita income of Brazilians and the high unemployment rate in the country.

Although there are still few organic chicken producers in Brazil, this market grows at surprising rates, based on information ob- tained from these producers. Korin, one of the main companies operating in this sector has been exporting organic chicken to Hong Kong since 2016. According to Korin, Hong Kong is one of the largest consumers of organic chicken in the world, being a demanding and well-informed market, paying a higher price for higher quality products. Also, according to the company, it was sought to produce antibiotic-free chickens, catering to animal wel- fare and based on natural agriculture. Korin carried out the first export of organic chickens from Brazil and other countries have already shown interest in buying their chickens, such as Peru, Colombia, Chile, Angola, United Arab Emirates, and some European
c)

countries, these in the study period. Korin grew 380% in the last 7 years and, in 2016, started its sales in the international mar- ket, exporting to Hong Kong, Europe, and the United Arab Emirates (Korin, 2020).

According to producers' association it is estimated that the vol- ume of organic production in Brazil is circa of 300 mil t/year, which generates a market of US$200 million/year. Most of the Brazilian organic production (80%) is in the South and Southeast region. Around 85% of production is exported, mainly to Europe, the United States, and Japan. The remaining (15%) is distributed on the domestic market (CPT, 2020). On the website of the Arab- Brazilian News Agency is available some information on the ex- port of organic chicken from Brazil to the United Arab Emirates (AnBA, 2020).

The organic food consumption in Brazil grows at a rate of 25% per year, with 17,000 certified properties in all the federa- tion units; 63% are exclusive organic producers that use around one million hectares. In 2018, organic products' consumption in Brazil was estimated at $1,029,866.12 (to the average dollar of $3,884), which requires regulation to assure consumers that they are purchasing an organic product, that is, a product that com- plies with legal regulations (Law No. 10,831 / 2003 and Decree No. 6,323 / 2007), established for organic agriculture in the country (Sebrae, 2020).
a) Operating Costs (OC): the ORG 600 system has the lowest Operating Cost ($13,430.47) when compared to the other production systems. The OC of the DH 2400 Broiler system,
i.e., is 2.8 times greater than the operating cost of the ORG 600 ($37,551.80/$13,430.47), see Table S7.

<list>b) NOPAT: the NOPAT value is produced by the difference be- tween NOR, OC, and taxes paid by the producer, therefore it identifies the profitability of the production system. In Ta- ble S7 can be seen that NOPAT obtained in the ORG 600 is 3.36 times greater than that obtained in the DH 2400 Broiler system ($80,072.58/$23,848.15), that is, the NOPAT magni- tude of the ORG 600 system is derived from a higher NOR and lower OC compared to the values identified in the DH 2400 Broiler system (items a and b, described above). 
c) Invested Capital (IC): the amount of Invested Capital in the ORG 600 system is much lower than that invested in other production systems. The DH 2400 Broiler system, for in- stance, is found to demand 2.35 times more IC than the ORG 600 system ($226,465.98/$96,519.65), see Table S7. 
d) Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): ROIC is a rate assem- bled by the relationship between NOPAT and invested cap- ital. Among the production systems analyzed the ORG 600 showed the highest NOPAT and the lowest capital invested, thus justifying the value of its ROIC (82.96%) significantly higher than the other rates of return, see Table S7. 
e) Cost of Invested Capital (CIC): the CIC depends on the amount of resources invested in the production system and on the attractiveness of the investment to the producer, called MARR. In this study, the MARR was determined at 6.75% (see Table S6), therefore, given the amount of IC and MARR, it is noted that the ORG 600 system had the lowest CIC ($65.511 X 0.0675 = $4,421.99)</list>

Based on the explanations given in the previous items, the EVA/kg of the ORG 600 production system is justified. The mag- nitude of its value ($1.123/kg) concerning the other systems is de- termined by the higher sale price of the product (24.75 and 4.53 times higher for the producer and the consumer, respectively), the lower operating cost ($13,430.47) and the lower demand for in- vested capital ($96,519.65). The higher price and lower cost in- crease profitability ($80,072.58). Higher profit with less demand for invested capital produces a higher rate of return (82.96%). A higher rate of return with a lower cost of capital increases the value of EVA/kg ($1.123).

For aquatic eutrophication, the results are similar to those for acidification (Fig. 2-c). The products that most affected this cate- gory were Heavy chickens, with emission values of 0.04 kg PO4 eq. per functional unit and with positive EVA values for DH aviaries and negative for conventional aviaries. In relation to the category global warming (100 yr) (Fig. 2-a) for DH systems and for conven- tional systems, the values were established between 1.3 kg CO2 eq and 1.6 kg CO2 eq. The amount of equivalent gases emitted in or- ganic systems by UF is almost the same as in the systems in com- parison, not exceeding 2%, that is 1.62 kg CO2 eq. for an average of 1.55 kg CO2 eq per UF. The EVA that the organic product generates represents approximately 80 times the EVA of the Broiler product in DH systems.

When results are normalized for each $ 1.00 EVA, it is possi- ble to note that organic systems are the lowest emitters regarding the global warming (100 yr) impact category (Table S8). The re- sults show that for each $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system emits 1,44 kg CO2 eq. These values very close to the PP 1200 Heavy and DH 2400 broiler systems. The difference is the number of FUs that each system needs to generate this economic value. In the second column of the same table, it is observed that to generate $ 1.00 of EVA, the organic system needs only one FU, whereas the DH 2400 Griller and DH 2400 Broiler systems need 83 and 71 FUs, respec- tively.
46: <header>3.2. Economic evaluation </header>

After environmental estimates, economic information (prices, costs and investments) related to the different types of chicken production systems addressed in this study were obtained. Re- garding the necessary investments, average prices considered were obtained in the market directly with the industries that supply

housing systems and equipment sales, earthworks, construction of sheds, and freight (Table S4). The value of buildings was converted into the average dollar rate for November 2017 ($ 3,257) and the value of housing systems was converted into average dollar rate for January 2018 ($ 3,213). Investment in working capital was also es- timated in order to finance the operational activities provided for in the production systems under study. The budgeted amount for the working capital investment was converted into average dollar rate for October 2017 ($ 3,196). Investment specifications can be read in (Table S11).
Table 4 shows summary information regarding the calculation of unit costs per chicken produced and per functional unit in kilo- grams. Detailed information is available in (Table S12).

It also happens that the volumes of firewood consumed by lots are similar when compared to the size of the aviaries. The aver- age price was $ 12.52 per cubic meter of firewood, and the vol- umes consumed varied from 0.010 m³ to 0.012 m³ of firewood per square meter. This small variation is due to the need for firewood for heat generation in the first 25 days of age of chicken in any of the analyzed systems.

The chicken litter, composed of wood shavings in the south of Brazil, is the most expensive item for the producer. In all produc- tion systems, the amount of shavings for bedding was ten centime- ters per square meter, with a replacement of another five centime- ters when passing six lots through the DH and conventional sys- items, and the same five centimeters for organic aviaries with re- placement every four lots.

Maintenance and insurance costs related to infrastructure are also absorbed by the producer. The insurance rate for poultry houses is 0.036% of the total value of housing systems, and for maintenance the percentage is on average 1%. The values of the Tax on Rural Territorial Property (ITR) were not available, therefore a rate of 0.2% was applied to the land price of an area of up to 50 hectares with 80% use based on the premise that most aviaries are located on small rural properties. The size of the area considered for this calculation was 20,000 m², and the average value of bare land consulted in the archives of the National Institute of Coloniza- tion and Agrarian Reform (INCRA) was $ 1,826.03 for the cities of the state of Paraná and $ 1,318.52 for the cities of the state of Santa Catarina.

Thus, the costs per functional unit produced in conventional poultry houses are close to the costs of DH poultry houses. The decimal difference is greater when compared to the volumes pro- duced annually. When comparing the cost of organic chicken with Griller chicken, there is a significant difference (300%) in the case of the 71-day organic product. The earned costs increase over time so that each lot reaches slaughter weight. There is a singularity for aviaries that produce organic chickens, where labor is the most expressive item due to the average slaughter period of 71 days, ac- cumulating approximately 2.4 wages per unit of work/month. Re- garding the poultry for organic production, it is noted that the electricity costs are much lower than the others, even with the amount paid of $ 0.16/kWh versus the $ 0.11 paid by producers in Paraná. Two reasons explain this lower cost. The first reason is
